content:
Automatic conversion of documents into numeric vectors predates modern deep learning. **Salton's vector space model (1970s)** treated documents as vectors of term weights—foundation for both search and later ML pipelines.

**TF–IDF weighting** (Spark Jones, 1972; refined in SMART and Okapi systems) became the standard way to emphasize discriminative terms. Bag-of-words with TF–IDF powered **decades of IR and text classification benchmarks**.

**scikit-learn** (2010 onward) unified these ideas under the **Transformer API**, making `CountVectorizer`, `TfidfVectorizer`, and `HashingVectorizer` composable with `Pipeline` and cross-validation—the pattern this topic treats as canonical.

Neural **word embeddings** and **transformer language models** largely replaced sparse vectors for accuracy on many NLP tasks, but sparse extractors remain essential for **baselines**, **interpretability**, **CPU-only deployment**, and **hybrid features** in production tabular+text systems.

timeline.label: Vector space model
timeline.description: Documents represented as term-weight vectors enable cosine similarity search and early statistical text processing.

timeline.label: TF–IDF standardization
timeline.description: Inverse document frequency down-weights ubiquitous terms; becomes default weighting for lexical retrieval.

timeline.label: Bag-of-words ML era
timeline.description: Naive Bayes, SVMs, and logistic regression on sparse matrices dominate text classification competitions and spam filtering.

timeline.label: sklearn Transformer API
timeline.description: fit/transform vectorizers integrate with Pipeline, GridSearchCV, and model persistence as first-class ML components.

timeline.label: Neural encoders
timeline.description: Embeddings and LLMs provide contextual features; sparse extractors remain baseline and interpretability tools in pipelines.
